response:
Short answer
- The 502s were transient Application Gateway (AGW) gateway-level failures concentrated in a few short windows — not a WAF regression and not backend 500s from the MCP service.

What we found
- AGW access logs for fazure-mcp showed 0 HTTP 500s from the backend, but multiple HTTP 502 responses concentrated around 13:46–13:50, 14:14–14:15 and especially 14:38–14:51 UTC.
- AGW firewall logs showed trusted Rackspace/Janus traffic matching the AllowTrustedVpnFazureMcp scope, so the WAF ruleset itself had not regressed.
- The small number of 403s were expected behaviour from the custom VpnOnly rule; during the investigation we discovered a few legitimate Janus/AWS and one Rackspace IP that were missing from the trusted allowlist and added them (this resolved the 403s caused by VpnOnly).

In short: the 502 spike was an AGW-level/transient gateway issue (not backend 500s or WAF regression). Separately, some POSTs were being blocked as 403s by the VpnOnly rule until missing trusted IPs were added.

In AGWAccessLogs for fazure-mcp.mscloud.rackspace.com over the prior 24h, there were 0 HTTP 500 responses, multiple 502s concentrated around 13:46-13:50 UTC and 14:14-14:15 UTC and especially 14:38-14:51 UTC, and a small number of 403s. AGWFirewallLogs showed trusted Rackspace/Janus traffic matching AllowTrustedVpnFazureMcp and current 403s were mainly expected", "2026-04-14: On 2026-04-14, investigated a high-CPU alert on VM wisdm-poc1-windows in RG wisdm-poc-platform. Key findings: (1) a Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/runCommand/action at 2026-04-14T20:09:39Z was executed by the managed identity of Logic App stress-test-controller and its response body showed action=stop for all CPU-lab VMs; this was not the cause of the incident. (2) Azure Monitor metrics showed the incident began around 2026-04-14T19:12Z with a short high inbound-network burst (~16.4 MB/s at 1", "2026-04-14: On 2026-04-14, after new MCP 403s from Janus AWS IPs, added the full Janus AWS address set 52.39.2.74, 52.41.136.221, 52.10.115.132, and 52.40.141.99 to the trusted IP allowlists for fazure MCP and oauth endpoints on Azure Application Gateway. Root cause was that recent POST /mcp failures on fazure-mcp.mscloud.rackspace.com at 2026-04-14T17:51:41Z through 2026-04-14T18:06:13Z were blocked by custom rule VpnOnly under policy scope fazure-mcp, even though those IPs are the known JanusAWS set alrea", "2026-04-14: On 2026-04-14, added Rackspace IP 72.4.120.196 to the trusted IP allowlists on Azure Application Gateway after confirming it had legitimate-looking internal traffic patterns. It was making successful requests to phoenix.mscloud.rackspace.com with PowerShell but its POST /mcp requests to fazure-mcp.mscloud.rackspace.com on 2026-04-13T20:18:02Z-20:18:05Z were blocked by custom rule VpnOnly because 72.4.120.196 was missing from the Rackspace trusted IP list. Live WAF rules updated: site policy waf-", "2026-04-13: On 2026-04-13 in /Users/benj8956/Downloads/Wisdm-POC/azr-failure-labs-demo, fixed the AKS operator page refresh path for active ACI runners. Root causes: /api/aks was classifying runners from the ACI containerGroups list payload, which often omitted full instanceView/currentState data, so activeRunner went null after refresh; and runner logs from ACI came back as timestamp-prefixed JSON lines, so the browser formatter treated them as raw text. Fixes: in failure-lab-portal/api/app.py, list_aks_ru", "2026-04-13: On 2026-04-13 in /Users/benj8956/Downloads/Wisdm-POC/azr-failure-labs-demo while patching the AKS operator page, a live deployment issue and a UI correctness fix were confirmed. The failure-lab portal deploy script depends on the Static Web Apps CLI, which now requires Node 18+; when the shell PATH resolved to Node 16.15.1, the API zip deploy succeeded but the Static Web App front-end publish failed, leaving old aks.html/aks.js live until redeployed with PATH=$HOME/.nvm/versions/node/v22.20.0/bi", "2026-04-13: Generated a fleet-wide prod report for Agent Fleet Platform covering 2026-04-11 14:28:59 UTC to 2026-04-13 14:28:59 UTC. DB-backed findings: 20,317 eventgrid_resource_events across 230 subscriptions; 713 actionable, 19,604 non-actionable, 0 redeliveries. Processing status: 19,924 captured_no_action, 393 completed. Top event skip reasons: unsupported_resource_type_write 13,373; delete_event_not_actioned 5,398; vm_os_unresolved 523; resource_action_cooldown_active 320; service_block_mismatch 286; ", "2026-04-10: On 2026-04-10 in /Users/benj8956/Downloads/Wisdm-POC, fixed the dedicated AKS operator page data path after live rollout. Root causes were all in failure-lab-portal/api/app.py: the /api/aks cluster-state command used brittle kubectl jsonpath expressions that failed under AKS Run Command shell semantics, latest_aks_runner preferred an old manual container group by lexicographic name sorting, and run_aks_command treated AKS managedClusters/runCommand as a synchronous ARM call instead of a long-run", "2026-04-10: On 2026-04-10 in /Users/benj8956/Downloads/Wisdm-POC while deploying/testing the AKS failure lab in wisdm-poc-platform, several live issues were found and fixed. The demo-api baseline was unstable because the inline Python server in aks-failure-lab/manifests/demo-api.yaml used HTTPServer and did not handle BrokenPipeError under kubelet probes; switching to ThreadingHTTPServer, explicit /healthz handling, HTTP/1.0, Connection: close, and swallowed BrokenPipeError/ConnectionResetError restored a h"]}
